english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
ugf_response: First we look at the first idea that you should give a thing if a person does not give. Then we see what happens when other people do not give. a simple plan says give the same thing no matter how much you give or not. If giving the same thing gives more than not giving you get back and less than giving. The second idea makes you keep more when it gets small. If taking away is better than getting nothing then taking away is always worse than giving. a simple picture of two parts is: give the same number of money and the person who gives gets the same pay. So if you care about the number of money you think you can take any number more than nothing and give nothing. If you do not care about the same as another you will want to take even if other people give and you end up with less than before. So the first idea is the best way to act with no new give while still letting others give because this fits the idea of a just share. Some might say that a simple table is enough but the numbers show that most people care about keeping their own part, so the best answer is to give the same size of money no matter what. So my answer is: The best plan works by making even share and letting others give, which shows why both things stay true.
